policy_statement: Transportation and Land Use: by 2027, preserve and produce 135,000 housing units including 60,000 affordable units, with 75% of new housing within 1/2 mile of activity centers and corridors.
mechanism_description: Concentrating new housing near activity centers and corridors supports shorter trips and higher transit/walking use, reducing vehicle miles traveled and related transportation emissions while shaping lower-carbon land use patterns.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Compact, transit-supportive land use reduces per-capita energy and material demand for transportation (less fuel use/vehicle-km traveled) and often lowers infrastructure intensity per person (shorter networks and less road/parking area), yielding the same mobility/access with less resource input.
primary_causal_pathway: Land-use + mobility policy → mode shift + reduced trip length/VMT → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Policies steer housing/jobs/services and street design/parking/TDM toward transit corridors and activity centers and improve transit performance, making non-auto travel more convenient than driving.  
(2) Mediating process: Increased proximity and accessibility shortens trips and increases transit/walk/bike mode share; parking constraints and commuter benefits further reduce driving demand.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced vehicle miles traveled and fuel combustion, lowering CO₂ (and other) emissions from on-road transportation; secondarily reduces energy/resource intensity of mobility and associated infrastructure.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from fewer and shorter car trips due to concentrating development around high-quality transit and improving non-auto access.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: TOD changes where development occurs and how people travel, leading to fewer/shorter car trips and thus lower transportation fuel combustion and GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation is primary: The direct, intended, and largest climate effect across TOD implementations is emissions reduction from avoided driving and induced mode shift; this outweighs other co-benefits in typical TOD policy designs.  
3) Inherent secondary chain(s):  
   - Resource Efficiency: Delivering access/mobility with fewer vehicle-km and typically less road/parking provision reduces energy and material throughput per capita.  
4) Edge cases applied: Some TOD can increase construction emissions or local congestion, but the mechanism as defined is centered on reducing auto dependence and shaping lower-carbon travel/land use; those countervailing effects are context-specific and not the dominant mechanism.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy genuinely implementing TOD to shift travel and concentrate growth around transit has mitigation as the core outcome (transport GHG reduction); resource-efficiency effects follow inherently from reduced VMT/auto dependence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “TOD” is implemented without improved transit service or without constraints on parking/auto access, mode shift may be weaker; however, the canonical mechanism still targets transport-emissions reduction via transit-centered land use, so Mitigation remains the correct primary category. Construction-phase embodied carbon impacts are possible but are not inherent to the TOD→transport-emissions reduction pathway.
key_indicators: “By 2027”, “preserve and produce 135,000 housing units”, “including 60,000 affordable housing units”, “75% of new housing”, “within ½ mile of … activity centers and corridors”
co_benefits: Housing affordability and supply; potential reduced household transportation costs and improved access to services (not framed as hazard exposure reduction in the text).
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could be interpreted as a planning-strategy because it embeds a location-efficiency siting rule; classified as target-setting because the core content is a quantified production/preservation target with a measurable proximity share.
